content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

AIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
100 K Street
Mento 95814
322-5660

October 7, 1975

Mr. William P. Baker
Executive Vice President
Contra Costa Taxpayers Association
Martinez, California 94553

Re: Request for Opinion
    No. 75-181

Dear Mr. Baker:

     Thank you for your letter of September 19, 1975,
requesting an opinion with regard to what constitutes
an expenditure in furtherance of the circulation of a
statewide petition under the Political Reform Act.

     The Commission recently adopted a regulation,
2 Cal. Adm. Code § 18550, clarifying the definition
of expenditure in Government Code § 85200 et seq. I
am enclosing a copy of this regulation, and since it
may answer your question, no formal opinion will be
issued in response to your request. If, however, you
conclude that the regulation does not solve your problem,
please feel free to resubmit your opinion request.

     I hope the foregoing is helpful. If you have any
further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the Commission

By
     Kenneth Finney
     Director, Legal Division

KF:gp
Enc.

CONTRA COSTA TAXPAYERS ASSOCIATION
P.O BOX 27 / 929 MAIN STREET · MARTINEZ, CALIFORNIA 94553 / PHONE 223-8810

September 13, 1975


Fair Political Practices Commission
925 J Street, Suite 522
Sacramento, Calif. 95814


Gentleman:

Pursuant to Section 83114 of the Government Code,
we are requesting an opinion as to whether the enclosed
newsletter would be considered a chargeable expense
under Government Code Section 83220.

It is the opinion of our counsel, Gordon, Waltz,
DeFraga, Watrous and Pozzaglia, that the subject matter
of the Taxpayers Association's monthly newsletter is
routine, and not a chargeable expense and does not need
the approval of proponents before the date of publication.

Your opinion on this matter is requested.


Sincerely,

William P. Baker
Executive Vice President


WPB:mc
Encl.

State Tax Initiatives

SHOULD IT BE TOUGHER TO TAX?

In its closing hours, the State Legislature voted to place on the June 1976
primary ballot a constitutional amendment which would allow the legislature, by
a simple majority vote, to increase any state tax or to levy a new tax.

Currently, the Constitution requires that any measure to increase bank,
corporation and insurance company taxes must pass by a 2/3 vote of the Legislature.
A bill increasing your income tax, sales taxes, inheritance and gift taxes and
cigarette and liquor taxes, requires only a simple majority vote of the Legislature.
Curiously, all segments of the taxing public are not treated equally.

The Santa Cruz Taxpayers Association believes that raising your taxes
should require an overwhelming concern for a higher tax on a bare majority. Since the
middle 1960's the sales tax has been increased by 50% and the personal income tax
has doubled.

Now petitions are being circulated throughout the state to place a rival
initiative on the June ballot. This proposal also equalizes tax voting require-
ments, but by requiring a 2/3 vote to pass all tax measures. In other words,
rather than making it easier to raise everyone's taxes, as the legislature's
proposal would do, the 2/3 initiative makes it more difficult to increase your
taxes. The 2/3 initiative does not affect procedures for setting tax rates for
the Unemployment Compensation or State Disability Funds.

If the initiative qualifies for the June ballot, the issue will be drawn.
The voter will have a clear choice: a 2/3 vote to raise everyone's taxes or a
simple majority. Which do you think will offer more protection to California
taxpayers against ever-increasing taxes?

Also included in the 2/3 amendment is a repeal of the property tax exemption
for insurance company's home offices ($24 million). This elimination of exemp-
tion has been passed by the Legislature (SCA-12) so it is not a critical part of
the 2/3 initiative.

CHOOSING SIDES

The Contra Costa Taxpayers Association has endorsed the 2/3 initiative along
with the California Taxpayers Association, the California Chamber of Commerce,
the California Association of Realtors, the California Manufacturers Association
and the California Farm Bureau Federation -- all organizations which oppose
higher taxes.

On the other side, favoring the simple majority referendum are the State
Legislature (which earlier this year refused to place the 2/3 rule on the ballot
to give voters a choice), the California Teachers Association, the League of
Women Voters, and People's Lobby. These organizations have rarely opposed tax
increases and have often favored increased government spending.

The liberal Modesto Bee recently (7-17-75) raised its hands in horror at
the thought of making it more difficult to raise taxes. According to the Bee
editorial, the 2/3 initiative "would lock into the State Constitution severe
limits on the ability of the state government to respond to changing economic
conditions and changing patterns of federal financing" -- that's longhand for
increasing your taxes.

In the next paragraph, the Bee says it more plainly. "A provision in the
Constitution which would require a two-thirds vote on every tax bill would make
it nearly impossible to raise or lower taxes."

Contrary to the Bee's opinion, every major tax increase since 1959 has
passed by a 2/3 vote of the legislature. Thus, what the Bee claims is a substantial
agreement among members of both political parties that a tax increase is needed,
it will be passed. In fact, since 1966, the state budget has increased from
$4.3 billion to $11.5 billion. It doesn't take much imagination to realize
what will happen when it's easier to raise everyone's taxes.

VOTER PROTECTIONS

The 2/3 initiative, if approved by the voters, would bring voting require-
ments for raising taxes into line with constitutional requirements that all
appropriations be approved by a 2/3 legislative vote. If the simple majority
on the initiative passes, they would only be proposing that it
would only be a matter of time before its legislature proposed reducing from
2/3 to a simple majority the vote needed to spend money-- your money.

Finally, the 2/3 vote initiative will make taxation legislation more
consistent with other "urgency" legislation which also requires a 2/3 vote
and goes into effect immediately upon passage.

SIGNATURES NEEDED

In order that California voters may have a choice in voting on taxation
policy, it is estimated that nearly a million signatures are needed to
qualify the Constitutional 2/3 vote initiative for the ballot.

It is estimated that 40,000 signatures are needed in Contra Costa County
alone to help meet this goal.

THE PLUCKED GOOSE

In the 18th century when Louis XIV of France faced huge demands for new
revenue to support his court, his building program and his wars, he found
success through a fiscal genius, J. B. Colbert, his finance minister.

According to Colbert, "The art of taxation consists of so plucking the
goose as to obtain the largest amount of feathers with the least possible
amount of hissing."

The 2/3 vote tax initiative seeks to increase the amount of hissing
from California taxpayers.

If you would like more information about this issue, please contact the
Contra Costa Taxpayers Association at 223-5310.
